name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, with approximately 30 million net tons of annual steelmaking capacity. Through four major acquisitions (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, Ferrous Processing & Trading, and Stelco), the company has transformed from an iron ore miner into a vertically integrated steel and mining company serving automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing markets.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed a decade-long transformation from pure-play iron ore miner to North America's largest integrated flat-rolled steel producer through four major acquisitions — AK Steel (2020), ArcelorMittal USA ($1.4B, 2020), Ferrous Processing & Trading (2022), and Stelco (2024) [1]. The company also built the only Hot Briquetted Iron (HBI) facility in the Great Lakes region, a meaningful process innovation for lower-carbon steelmaking. However, the cancellation of the $500M green steel project in 2025 tempers the innovation narrative [4]. AI Deployment (1/2): In May 2026, Cleveland-Cliffs announced a three-year strategic partnership with Palantir Technologies to deploy AI across its operational and commercial systems — production planning, order entry, inventory optimization, and predictive maintenance [5]. The partnership is live but no quantified cost savings or revenue gains have been reported yet; this is early-stage deployment, not shipping AI products to customers. Market Share (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is the undisputed leader in North American flat-rolled steel, with ~30 million net tons of annual capacity and dominant share in automotive-grade steel supply to major OEMs [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): The POSCO partnership for trade-compliant EV-grade advanced high-strength steel remains at MOU stage with significant valuation disagreements reported as of May 2026 — POSCO cited 'different opinions' as the reason for stalled progress [6]. No other specific next-gen products with firm launch dates. R&D Intensity (0/1): Steel industry R&D spending is structurally low as a percentage of revenue; Cleveland-Cliffs does not report a meaningful R&D line item.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs's stated mission is to be 'the most profitable, environmentally and socially responsible, and customer-preferred supplier of iron ore and steel products' — clear in identifying what the company does and for whom, but not distinctive or purpose-driven beyond operational excellence [3]. It could be any industrial company's mission statement. Part B — Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is a major employer across steel communities in Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and numerous mill towns where it is often the largest private employer. The company publishes a sustainability report and supports local community programs. However, no specific charitable foundation, dollar amounts, or distinctive community investment programs were identified beyond standard corporate citizenship. Employee Retention (0/2): No public employee retention or voluntary turnover data available. The workforce is heavily unionized, which provides structural stability but does not constitute evidence of mission-driven retention per IOWN criteria. Insider Buying (0/2): Significant net insider selling in the last 12 months. CEO Lourenco Goncalves sold 3,000,000 shares through a grantor retained annuity trust on February 11, 2026, valued at $37.2M at a weighted average price of $12.42/share [7]. CFO Celso Goncalves sold 214,308 shares valued at $2.87M on June 5, 2026 [7]. Over the last 12 months, insiders sold 3.12M shares ($38M) versus purchasing only 23,500 shares ($243K) — overwhelmingly net selling.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics for a Growth-sleeve stock. Q1 2026 showed turnaround momentum: consolidated revenues of $4.92B (up 6.3% YoY from $4.63B), adjusted EBITDA of $95M (up $274M YoY), and steel average selling price of $1,048/net ton [1][2]. However, the company remains unprofitable with negative ROE of -18.6% and negative profit margin of -6.4%, reflecting the hangover from a catastrophic FY2025 that produced a $1.4B GAAP net loss. D/E of 129.3% is elevated from acquisition financing but well below the 300% floor. Forward P/E of 27.35 reflects market expectation of a return to profitability — management guided for positive free cash flow by Q2 2026. The terminated slab contract is expected to deliver ~$500M in incremental EBITDA, and locked-in coal contracts should save >$100M annually [2]. Five-year revenue CAGR of ~12% reflects the transformative acquisition period. Liquidity floor check: 18,147,633 avg daily volume x $13.71 = $248.8M daily liquidity — comfortably passes. Metric calculation (Growth weights, PEG excluded as null): D/E 0.74 (1x) + ROE 0.00 (2x) + Rev Growth YoY 0.88 (1x) + Rev Growth 5yr CAGR 1.00 (2x) + Fwd P/E 0.82 (1x) + Profit Margin 0.00 (1x) = 4.44/8.0 = 5.5, rounded to 6.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Steel manufacturing is a fundamentally physical-world, capital-intensive industry that AI structurally cannot displace. Converting iron ore to steel via blast furnaces and electric arc furnaces, managing 30 million tons of annual capacity across multiple integrated facilities, and serving automotive OEMs with precision-grade flat-rolled products requires physical infrastructure, metallurgical expertise, and supply chain relationships that are immune to AI substitution [1]. No analysts cite AI as a displacement risk for Cleveland-Cliffs's core business. More significantly, AI infrastructure buildout (data centers, power generation, electrical grid expansion) is a direct demand driver for structural steel and electrical steel products — AI increases demand for what Cleveland-Cliffs makes [8]. The primary business risks remain cyclical (steel prices, automotive demand, tariffs, Chinese overcapacity), not technological. Scoring at 8 rather than 9 because some back-office and commercial functions face efficiency displacement, though this is marginal relative to the core physical operations. AI Opportunity (0/1): The Palantir partnership announced in May 2026 is focused on internal operational optimization — production planning, predictive maintenance, and inventory management [5]. This is AI as a cost tool, not an AI revenue stream. Cleveland-Cliffs generates zero revenue from AI products or services.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves has demonstrated genuine existential flexibility in transforming from an iron ore miner to the dominant U.S. flat-rolled steelmaker — a bold multi-year reinvention. However, Goncalves's combative competitive posture toward US Steel, his reliance on tariff protection as competitive strategy, massive personal share sales even as he advocates for the company's future, and the cancellation of the green steel project when costs mounted all point to a company playing to win the current game rather than to keep playing indefinitely. Sinek would note the gap between the bold strategic vision and the finite competitive behavior.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Goncalves articulates a vision of rebuilding American steel manufacturing and domestic supply chain security — genuine, service-oriented, and specific to CLF's competitive position. His advocacy for steel tariffs and domestic procurement rules reflects a consistent decade-long conviction [3]. However, Sinek would classify this as a cause that exists but is shallow: 'make American steel dominant again' is finite in framing — it defines victory in competitive terms rather than describing a future state that can never be fully achieved. The mission statement ('most profitable, customer-preferred supplier') is operational, not aspirational. Leadership references market share and competitive positioning more than purpose in earnings calls. Score: 1 point (cause exists but is shallow), display 4.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team through four major acquisitions without catastrophic cultural failures — integrating AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, and Stelco workforces is a meaningful organizational achievement [1]. His stated respect for unions ('You don't need to be a union company to be a good company, but it's very easy to be a bad company when you don't have a union') reflects genuine regard for worker voice [3]. However, Goncalves's famously combative personality — confrontational on earnings calls, publicly aggressive toward competitors and analysts — creates tension with the psychological safety Sinek prizes. Board changes in 2026 (Lead Director departure, committee reshuffling) suggest governance dynamics under pressure. The 30,000-employee organization spans multiple legacy cultures still being integrated. Score: 1 point (adequate trust), display 6.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's public language toward competitors is textbook finite game thinking by Sinek's definition. His hostile pursuit of US Steel, combative rhetoric against foreign steelmakers, and strategic reliance on tariffs to block competition rather than improve products all reflect a 'compete to destroy' mindset rather than 'compete to learn' [3]. He frames the competitive landscape in zero-sum terms — winning market share, blocking imports, defeating rivals — rather than using competitors' strengths to reveal CLF's own weaknesses. Sinek would cite this as a cautionary example. Score: 0 points (finite competitive mindset), display 3.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The transformation from iron ore miner to integrated steelmaker is genuinely significant strategic flexibility — Cleveland-Cliffs fundamentally reinvented its business model through four acquisitions over four years, entering an entirely different industry position [1]. The HBI facility and automotive-grade steel capabilities represent meaningful diversification from commodity products. However, this flexibility has limits: the cancellation of the $500M green steel project in 2025 when costs mounted, and the stalled POSCO partnership due to valuation disagreements, suggest the company's willingness to reinvent itself ends when the financial cost becomes real [4][6]. Sinek would note this is flexibility driven by market opportunity rather than by a Just Cause, which is why it stalls under pressure. Score: 1 point (some flexibility), display 6.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves's acquisition of ArcelorMittal USA for $1.4B during COVID-19 required extraordinary financial courage — betting the company's balance sheet during a pandemic-era demand collapse [1]. Maintaining capital expenditure commitment through the catastrophic 2025 downcycle ($1.4B GAAP net loss, $37M adjusted EBITDA) while competitors cut demonstrates willingness to sacrifice short-term results. However, the $37.2M in personal share sales by Goncalves in February 2026, even as he publicly advocates for the turnaround story, creates a gap between stated conviction and personal behavior that Sinek would flag [7]. The cancelled green steel project shows pragmatism winning over long-term capability investment when costs mount. Score: 1 point (some courageous decisions), display 6.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is showing early turnaround momentum after a catastrophic FY2025. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.92B beat estimates by 1.8%, adjusted EBITDA surged to $95M (up $274M YoY), and steel average selling price rose to $1,048/net ton on better product mix and higher HRC prices [1][2]. The terminated slab contract is the single most powerful lever — management estimates ~$500M in incremental annual EBITDA from ending the value-destructive ArcelorMittal slab supply agreement, plus >$100M in annual savings from locked-in coal contracts [2]. Management guided full-year 2026 volumes of 16.5-17.0 million tons with realized prices ~$60/ton higher than 2025, and expects a return to profitability and positive free cash flow by Q2 2026. At $13.71 with a $7.1B market cap, the stock trades at 27.35x forward earnings — a cyclical recovery multiple that could compress rapidly if EBITDA targets materialize. The 50% steel tariff provides meaningful pricing protection for domestic producers, and the Palantir AI partnership signals operational modernization [5][8].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution risk and balance sheet fragility. FY2025 produced a $1.4B GAAP net loss with only $37M adjusted EBITDA — the 2026 recovery requires every identified catalyst to materialize simultaneously [2]. D/E of 129.3% leaves limited flexibility if the turnaround lags; any refinancing in a higher-rate environment would pressure an already burdened balance sheet. The POSCO partnership — potentially the most transformative catalyst — is stalled, with POSCO citing 'different opinions' on valuation as of May 2026 and no deadline set for resolution [6]. Profit margin remains negative at -6.4% and ROE at -18.6%, meaning the company is currently destroying shareholder value. An $80M one-time energy cost spike in Q1 2026 caused the stock to fall 6% post-earnings despite the headline beat, revealing how fragile market confidence remains [2]. CEO Goncalves's $37.2M in personal share sales in February 2026, even as he publicly advocates for the turnaround, raises alignment concerns [7]. Automotive demand (30% of revenue) faces EV transition uncertainty, and Chinese steel overcapacity remains a structural global headwind even with tariff protection.
key_catalysts: Slab contract termination delivering estimated $500M incremental annual EBITDA plus >$100M in coal contract savings, representing the single largest financial improvement lever for FY2026 profitability [2]

Potential POSCO partnership finalizing at $700M+ investment for 10%+ equity stake, providing balance sheet relief, access to EV-grade advanced high-strength steel technology, and validation of CLF's strategic position [6]

50% U.S. steel tariff providing sustained pricing protection for domestic flat-rolled producers, with management guiding realized prices ~$60/ton above 2025 levels across 16.5-17.0 million tons [2][8]
key_risks: Negative ROE of -18.6% and profit margin of -6.4% mean the company is currently unprofitable — the entire investment thesis depends on a turnaround that has not yet been proven beyond one quarter of modest EBITDA improvement [1][2]
POSCO partnership stalled over valuation disagreements with no deadline for resolution, removing the most significant potential balance sheet catalyst and EV-grade technology pathway [6]

CEO Goncalves and CFO Celso Goncalves sold a combined $40M in shares in early 2026 while the stock traded near multi-year lows, creating an insider conviction gap that undermines the bullish turnaround narrative [7]
